The PR adds an optional `tools.exec.deniedPaths` config surface, exec preflight path matching across gateway/sandbox/node, schema/docs wiring, config normalization changes, and regression tests.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible. The PR head strips `C:\**` to `C:`, and Node's win32 resolver treats `C:` as drive-relative to the workdir, so drive-root denied patterns can miss absolute operands elsewhere on that drive.
commands.review_followup: There is a narrow, actionable PR-branch repair: fix Windows drive-root denied pattern handling and add one regression without changing the overall config surface.

target.title: fix(exec): block configured denied path operands
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: issue #11829 calls out agent access to provider/API key material as a security roadmap gap, and the issue discussion proposed a configurable `tools.exec.deniedPaths` surface for tenant-controlled secret paths.
- Solution: add `tools.exec.deniedPaths` as an exec preflight guard that rejects commands referencing configured paths such as `/run/secrets/**` before gateway/node/sandbox execution. The guard now inspects command-carrier and shell-wrapper payloads, sandbox checks resolve relative operands in the container path namespace, node-host checks run after node target resolution using the target node platform namespace, config loading preserves authored denied-path `~` patterns until execution, home-relative denied patterns such as `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` resolve against the selected exec HOME, and `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands are expanded before denied-path matching.
- What changed: runtime guard, global/agent config wiring, zod/type/schema help/labels/tags, docs, generated config baseline, config path normalization, and regression tests for direct paths, shell-wrapper payloads, command substitutions, sandbox container-relative traversal, node-host Windows path semantics, home-relative denied patterns, and HOME-prefixed command operands.
- What did NOT change: no changes to model catalog filtering, auth proxy docs, or the existing models.json key-stripping work already covered by other PRs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #11829
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

- This gives operators a config-level guard for mounted secret directories, especially deployments that keep provider credentials in predictable paths such as `/run/secrets/**`. It is defense-in-depth rather than a replacement for sandboxing or secret isolation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: exec commands can currently reference mounted sensitive paths unless deployment isolation catches the access; after this patch a configured denied path is rejected before host execution.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node 22.22.0, invoking the real `createExecTool` runtime with `host: "gateway"`, `security: "full"`, `ask: "off"`, and `deniedPaths: ["/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/**"]`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx` importing `./src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts`, creating the exec tool, and calling `tool.execute(...)` for these commands:
  - `cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"`
  - `bash -lc 'cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"'`
  - `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key")"'`
- Evidence after fix (copied live local runtime output):

```text
blocked: cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
blocked: bash -lc 'cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key"'
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
blocked: bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "/tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key")"'
Security Violation: exec command references denied path /tmp/openclaw-denied-proof/provider.key
```

- Observed result after fix: the exec tool throws before running each command, including the shell-wrapper and command-substitution forms, so the denied path is not handed to gateway execution.
- Additional sandbox namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` now covers `host: "sandbox"`, `workdir: "/workspace"`, `deniedPaths: ["/run/secrets/**"]`, and `cat ../../run/secrets/provider.key`. The preflight resolves the operand against `/workspace` using POSIX container semantics and rejects `/run/secrets/provider.key` before `buildExecSpec` is called.
- Additional node namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts` now covers `host: "node"` after a Windows node target resolves with `platform: "win32"`, `workdir: "C:\\Work"`, `deniedPaths: ["C:\\Secrets\\**"]`, and `type C:\\Secrets\\provider.key`. The node path preflight rejects the command before `system.run` is called. It also covers case-insensitive Windows matching with `type c:\\secrets\\provider.key`.
- Additional home-relative proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` covers `deniedPaths: ["~/.openclaw/credentials/**"]` and blocks commands referencing the resolved HOME credential path via literal resolved paths, `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key`, and a shell-wrapper `${HOME}/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` payload. `src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts` verifies authored `tools.exec.deniedPaths` entries stay home-relative during generic config path normalization so the exec guard can resolve them later in the selected runtime namespace.
- Additional config-through-runtime namespace proof: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts` flows `normalizeConfigPaths({ tools: { exec: { deniedPaths: ["~/.openclaw/credentials/**"] } } })` into sandbox exec with `HOME=/workspace` and proves `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` is blocked as `/workspace/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key`. `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts` flows the same normalized config into a Windows node target with `HOME=C:\\Users\\agent` and proves `C:\\Users\\agent\\.openclaw\\credentials\\provider.key` is blocked before `system.run`.
- What was not tested: a full multi-tenant Docker deployment with real mounted provider secrets.
- Before evidence: the new regression tests failed before the follow-up fixes because `bash -lc 'cat "<deniedFile>"'`, `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "<deniedFile>")"'`, the sandbox container-relative traversal, the Windows node target command, the home-relative denied pattern, and `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands all resolved successfully with `(no output)` or left path prefixes unexpanded instead of rejecting.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: exec had approval/safe-bin/env guards, but no config-level path denylist for sensitive operand paths. The first implementation scanned only the outer argv tokens, used host path semantics for sandbox relative operands, checked node targets before the target platform was known, let generic config normalization consume authored home-relative denied patterns before runtime namespace selection, did not normalize home-relative denied patterns, and did not expand common `$HOME` / `${HOME}` command operands before matching.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test asserted that a configured secret path is blocked inside a shell wrapper, command substitution, sandbox container-relative path, Windows node target path, `~/...` denied pattern, or HOME-prefixed command operand before execution.
- Contributing context (if known): issue #11829 covers a broader roadmap; this PR implements only the `tools.exec.deniedPaths` part.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts`, `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts`
- Scenarios the tests lock in:
  - direct `cat "<deniedFile>"` is rejected;
  - `bash -lc 'cat "<deniedFile>"'` is rejected;
  - `bash -lc 'echo "$(cat "<deniedFile>")"'` is rejected;
  - sandbox `cat ../../run/secrets/provider.key` from `/workspace` is rejected as `/run/secrets/provider.key` before `buildExecSpec`;
  - node-host `type C:\Secrets\provider.key` is rejected after resolving the target node as `platform: "win32"` and before `system.run`;
  - node-host Windows denied-path matching is case-insensitive for `C:\Secrets\**` versus `c:\secrets\provider.key`;
  - `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` is runtime-resolved and blocks the selected HOME credential path;
  - config-loaded `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` stays authored through generic path normalization and blocks sandbox `HOME=/workspace`;
  - config-loaded `~/.openclaw/credentials/**` stays authored through generic path normalization and blocks Windows node `HOME=C:\Users\agent`;
  - `$HOME/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` and shell-wrapper `${HOME}/.openclaw/credentials/provider.key` operands are expanded before matching and rejected.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises `createExecTool` directly and proves the preflight guard runs even when approvals are bypassed.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config key: `tools.exec.deniedPaths`. Commands that reference matching paths now fail before exec starts.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
exec command -> env/path setup -> host execution may receive /run/secrets/provider.key

After:
exec command -> env/path setup -> denied path preflight -> Security Violation before host execution
```

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts` -> 119 passed
- `pnpm config:schema:check` -> ok
- `pnpm config:docs:check` -> ok
- `pnpm tsgo:core` -> ok
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/config/normalize-paths.ts src/config/normalize-paths.test.ts src/config/io.ts` -> all matched files use correct format
- `git diff --check` -> ok

target.title: docker: support optional pip packages in local builds
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Local Docker builds support optional apt packages, but there is no equivalent setup/Dockerfile hook for installing Python packages needed by operator-managed scripts or skills. - Solution: Add an opt-in `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES` build arg and pass it through `scripts/docker/setup.sh`. - What changed: Docker builds now install `python3-pip` only when pip packages are requested and `pip3` is missing, then install the requested pip packages with `pip3 install --no-cache-dir --break-system-packages`. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default Docker images and builds are unchanged when `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES` is unset. Existing apt package build args are not changed. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Motivation Local Docker builds already support opt-in apt packages for operator-specific runtime needs. Some operator-managed scripts and skills also need Python packages that are not available as part of the default image. This adds a matching opt-in pip package hook while keeping default builds unchanged. - ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Local Docker builds can now install opt-in Python packages through `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES`. - Real environment tested: Local Docker build from this branch using `scripts/docker/setup.sh`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: To run the docker setup with the new parameter: ```bash OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests humanize" ./scripts/docker/setup.sh ``` To test that the requested packages have been installed: ```bash docker run --rm --entrypoint python3 openclaw:local -c 'import requests, humanize; print(requests.__version__); print(humanize.naturalsize(1234567)); print(requests.__file__); print(humanize.__file__)' ``` - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): [1. Terminal Output.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978127/1.Terminal.Output.txt) [2. setup-pip-test.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978128/2.setup-pip-test.log) [3. import-test.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978129/3.import-test.log) - Observed result after fix: The resulting `openclaw:local` image includes the requested pip packages and can import them from Python. - What was not tested: Full live gateway startup from this branch was not completed; the setup script reached the image build step, but the temporary-user environment hit a local volume ownership error during the post-build setup phase. The Docker build-arg behavior was verified by importing the requested packages from the resulting image. ## Root Cause (if applicable) N/A. This is an opt-in Docker build customization hook, not a bug fix or regression. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A. No automated regression test added; this is a Docker build customization path and was verified with a real local Docker build. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can opt into installing Python packages in local Docker builds by setting `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES`. Default builds are unchanged when the variable is unset. ## Diagram (if applicable) N/A ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? Yes - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: When `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES` is explicitly set, Docker builds run `pip3 install` for the requested packages, which may download packages from the configured pip index. This is opt-in build-time behavior only; default builds are unchanged. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Linux - Runtime/container: Docker local build, `openclaw:local` - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests"` ### Steps 1. clone from the forked repo: ```bash git clone https://github.com/stephenredmond-straiteis/openclaw.git ``` 2. check out the branch: ```bash git checkout docker-pip-packages-build-arg ``` 4. Built the image with: ```bash OPENCLAW_SKIP_ONBOARDING=1 \ OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests humanize" \ ./scripts/docker/setup.sh 2>&1 | tee setup-pip-test.log ``` 5. Ran the Python test for the libraries: ```bash docker run --rm --entrypoint python3 openclaw:local -c 'import requests, humanize; print(requests.__version__); print(humanize.naturalsize(1234567)); print(requests.__file__); print(humanize.__file__)' | tee import-test.log ``` ### Expected - The requested pip package is installed in the built image and importable from Python. ### Actual - both requests and humanize imported successfully and reported version/path. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) [1. Terminal Output.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978127/1.Terminal.Output.txt) [2. setup-pip-test.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978128/2.setup-pip-test.log) [3. import-test.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27978129/3.import-test.log) # Human Verification (required) • Verified scenarios: Local Docker build with OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES="requests humanize"; verified requests can be imported in the resulting image. • Edge cases checked: Verified python3-pip is installed automatically when pip packages are requested and pip3 is unavailable in the base runtime image. • What you did not verify: Full gateway startup was not tested; the setup script reached the image build step, but the temporary-user environment hit local volume ownership errors during the post-build setup phase. # Review Conversations • [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. • [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. # Compatibility / Migration • Backward compatible? Yes • Config/env changes? Yes • Migration needed? No • If yes, exact upgrade steps: No migration required. Operators who want this behavior can set OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES during local Docker builds. # Risks and Mitigations • Risk: Requested pip packages can add build time and depend on external package availability. • Mitigation: The behavior is opt-in and only runs when OPENCLAW_DOCKER_PIP_PACKAGES is set.

target.title: fix(ui): keep delete confirm in viewport
target.body:
## Related issue

Related #50277

## Summary

- Position the chat delete confirmation popover relative to the viewport instead of the scroll container.
- Choose above or below the delete button based on available vertical space.
- Clamp the popover horizontally so it remains reachable on narrow viewports.
- Add focused jsdom geometry coverage for top-edge, bottom-edge, and narrow-width placement.

## User-visible behavior

The delete confirmation popover in Control UI chat should remain visible and reachable when deleting messages near the top or edges of the chat viewport.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI grouped chat delete confirmation could render out of reach when opened near the top or horizontal viewport edges because it was absolutely positioned inside the scrollable chat area. This patch positions the popover against the viewport, flips it above or below the delete button, and clamps it inside viewport margins.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `4a0465007c` on Raspberry Pi-class Linux host, Node `v22.21.1`, Chromium `142.0.7444.175`, using the PR's `.chat-delete-confirm` markup/CSS and viewport-placement code path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
chromium --headless=new --disable-gpu --no-sandbox --force-device-scale-factor=1 --window-size=360,640 --dump-dom file:///tmp/openclaw-delete-confirm-proof.html
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Terminal output excerpt from Chromium:

```text
OpenClaw Control UI delete-confirm viewport proof
viewport=500x553
top-edge: placement=below left=90 top=38 withinViewport=true
bottom-edge: placement=above left=12 top=86 withinViewport=true
horizontal-edge: placement=below left=104 top=130 withinViewport=true
```

- Observed result after fix: In Chromium, the delete confirmation popover remains inside the viewport margins for top-edge, bottom-edge, and horizontal-edge cases. The top-edge case opens below the trigger, the bottom-edge case opens above the trigger, and the horizontal-edge case stays within the right boundary.
- What was not tested: Full gateway-backed dashboard click path on this Raspberry Pi-class host; dependency installation was intentionally avoided. Visual screenshot capture was not attached because copied browser output is the supplied proof.

## Supplemental validation

- `git diff --check` exited 0 locally with no whitespace errors.
- GitHub Actions `checks-node-core-ui` passed on head `4a0465007c`.
- GitHub Actions `check-test-types` passed on head `4a0465007c` after adding `this: HTMLElement` to the test geometry mock.

## AI-assisted

AI-assisted: yes. I reviewed the diff and understand the change scope.

target.title: UI: add progressive disclosure for long markdown messages
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: When OpenClaw Control UI receives massive text payloads (e.g., 140k+ chars), the browser struggles to render the DOM, leading to UI freezing.
- Why it matters: Users lose the ability to inspect full payloads because the current fallback strategy hard-truncates text at 140,000 chars.
- What changed: Introduced a **Progressive Disclosure (Show More/Less)** component for long messages (>5,000 chars) and safely increased the underlying markdown parsing limits.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Did not change the 200-message chat history limit or the backend API truncation logic.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Extremely long messages will now render collapsed with a "Show more" button and a gradient fade.
- Clicking "Show more" expands the message and reveals a "Show less" button.
- Messages up to 500,000 chars can now be loaded into the UI (previously truncated at 140k).
- Auto-JSON detection limit lowered from 20k to 10k chars to prevent UI freezes.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js / Local web UI
- Model/provider: Any

### Steps

1. Start OpenClaw Control UI
2. Have an agent output a 150k+ character raw text dump.

### Expected

- Browser freezes or text is hard truncated with `... truncated`.

### Actual

- Text gracefully folds to 400px height with a "Show more" button.

## Evidence

- [x] Tested locally with `pnpm build` and `pnpm check`.
- [x] `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` passes.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Manually tested streaming a 100k character message.
- Edge cases checked: Auto-scrolling works perfectly while streaming. Tested toggling "Show more" and "Show less" repeatedly.
- What you did **not** verify: Did not verify edge cases on legacy browsers without CSS `mask-image` support (they will just see a hard cutoff).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the PR.
- Files/config to restore: `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts`, `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts`, `ui/src/styles/chat/text.css`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Unexpected collapsing of short messages or UI layout jumps during streaming.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: DOM Bloat (fully expanded 500k-character message still resides in the DOM).
  - Mitigation: The existing 200-message render limit (`CHAT_HISTORY_RENDER_LIMIT`) remains a crucial safety net.

target.title: docs(channels): add connection architecture overview and transport notes
target.body:
## What

Adds a **Connection architecture** section to `docs/channels/index.md` with a transport table covering all 22 supported channels across 10 transport categories. Also adds one-line transport notes to the Discord and Telegram channel pages.

## Why

The current docs explain how to *set up* each channel but not how the connection works. Users planning firewall rules, reverse proxy configurations, or debugging connectivity issues have no single reference for understanding which channels are outbound-only versus which require inbound infrastructure.

## Changes

- `docs/channels/index.md` — New "Connection architecture" section with transport table and practical guidance
- `docs/channels/discord.md` — One-line transport note (outbound WebSocket, no inbound infra needed)
- `docs/channels/telegram.md` — One-line transport note (long polling default, webhook optional)

## Transport classifications

All 22 channels mapped to 10 transport categories, verified against each channel's documentation and source code:

- **Outbound WebSocket:** Discord, Slack (Socket Mode), Mattermost, Feishu (default), Twitch (IRC protocol over WebSocket), Nostr
- **Outbound TCP/TLS:** IRC
- **Outbound HTTP:** Tlon (Urbit ship API)
- **Long polling:** Telegram (default), Zalo (default)
- **Inbound webhook:** Telegram (optional), Slack (HTTP Events), Feishu (optional), Google Chat, Microsoft Teams, LINE, Nextcloud Talk, Synology Chat, Zalo (optional)
- **Local REST + webhook:** BlueBubbles
- **Local CLI / JSON-RPC:** Signal (signal-cli), iMessage (legacy, imsg)
- **Linked session:** WhatsApp (Baileys), Zalo Personal
- **Gateway WebSocket:** WebChat (client connects to gateway)
- **Matrix SDK:** Matrix (outbound /sync)

Multi-transport channels (Telegram, Slack, Feishu, Zalo) noted in both the table and a callout. Mattermost's optional inbound HTTP for slash commands noted separately.

## AI-assisted PR 🤖

- [x] This PR is AI-assisted (authored by an OpenClaw agent running Claude Opus, directed by a human)
- [x] Testing: docs-only change, verified `oxfmt --check` passes locally
- [x] I (the human author) understand what the changes do
- [x] Codex GitHub review addressed (all P2 items resolved — see review history below)
- [x] Bot review conversations resolved

## Review history

This PR went through 4 rounds of automated review and iteration:

1. **v1 (#51501):** Greptile found WhatsApp misclassified, 4 channels missing, anchor link issue — closed
2. **v2 (#51504):** Fixed above, but force-push included unrelated files — auto-closed by barnacle bot
3. **v3 (initial `5e7ec7f`):** Clean branch, all Greptile and prior Codex feedback addressed. Claude Code (Opus) verified all 22 channels against source. Greptile 4/5.
4. **v4 (current `1c92df7`):** Addressed Codex P2 review comments:
   - Inbound webhook row: changed from "requires public HTTPS" to "depends — public or local network" (self-hosted channels like Nextcloud Talk don't need public endpoints)
   - Local CLI/JSON-RPC row: added "remote operation possible via SSH or HTTP" (iMessage supports remote Mac over SSH, Signal supports external HTTP daemon)
   - Mattermost: noted optional inbound HTTP for slash commands in \<Note\> block
   - Twitch: kept in Outbound WebSocket (confirmed `@twurple/chat` uses WebSocket transport) with "(IRC protocol over WebSocket)" parenthetical

target.title: fix(security): normalize Cyrillic and Greek confusable chars in boundary marker sanitization
target.body:
## Problem

The external content marker sanitization in `foldMarkerText` normalizes fullwidth ASCII characters and Unicode angle bracket homoglyphs before checking for boundary marker spoofs. However, it does not account for **Cyrillic and Greek confusable characters** that are visually identical to their Latin counterparts.

For example, Cyrillic `А` (U+0410), `С` (U+0421), `Е` (U+0415), `О` (U+041E), `Т` (U+0422), and `Х` (U+0425) are pixel-perfect matches for Latin A, C, E, O, T, and X in most fonts. An attacker could construct markers like `<<<ЕXТЕRNАL_UNТRUSТЕD_СОNТЕNТ>>>` (with Cyrillic letters substituted) that bypass the regex check while still being interpreted as boundary markers by downstream LLMs.

## Fix

- Add a `CONFUSABLE_LETTER_MAP` covering the most common Cyrillic uppercase/lowercase and Greek uppercase/lowercase confusable characters
- Apply confusable normalization in `foldMarkerChar` before the angle bracket check
- Extend the `foldMarkerText` regex character class to match these additional code points
- Add test cases verifying both Cyrillic and Greek confusable marker bypass attempts are caught

## Testing

- All 47 existing + 2 new tests pass in `external-content.test.ts`
- No changes to public API surface; purely additive to the internal normalization logic

target.title: fix(onboard): discover model context window from proxy /model/info API
target.body:
## Summary

- During onboard, copilot-proxy and LiteLLM providers hardcode `contextWindow: 128000` and `maxTokens: 8192` for all discovered models
- This causes premature context compaction for models with larger windows (e.g. `claude-opus-4.6-1m` routed through LiteLLM with 1M context), wasting ~87% of available capacity
- Fix: query the proxy /v1/model/info endpoint during setup to read actual max_input_tokens and max_output_tokens per model, falling back to previous defaults when the endpoint is unavailable

## Root Cause

LiteLLM exposes model capabilities via model_info in its config (e.g. max_input_tokens: 1000000 for Opus 4.6 1M), and serves this data at /v1/model/info. However, the onboard flow never queries this endpoint - it assigns the same 128k/8192 defaults to every model regardless of actual capability.

This means the context window guard triggers compaction at ~100k tokens even when the underlying model supports 1M, leading to unnecessary history truncation in long sessions.

## Changes

- **extensions/copilot-proxy/index.ts**: add fetchModelInfoMap() that queries /v1/model/info during onboard and passes discovered contextWindow/maxTokens to buildModelDefinition()
- **src/commands/onboard-auth.config-litellm.ts**: add fetchLitellmModelInfo() export and accept optional modelInfoOverrides in config appliers (keeps functions sync for type compatibility)
- **src/commands/auth-choice.apply.api-key-providers.ts**: call fetchLitellmModelInfo() before applying config, passing real values via closure

## Backward Compatibility

- When /v1/model/info is unavailable (proxy not running, non-LiteLLM proxy, network error), falls back silently to previous 128k/8192 defaults
- applyLitellmConfig and applyLitellmProviderConfig remain sync - the async fetch happens in the caller
- Non-interactive onboard path continues using defaults (LiteLLM may not be running during non-interactive setup)

## Test plan

- [x] pnpm tsgo passes (type check)
- [x] onboard-auth.config-shared.test.ts - 6/6 pass
- [x] onboard-auth.test.ts - 31/31 pass
- [x] onboard-custom.test.ts - 29/29 pass
- [x] Verified locally: with LiteLLM running, opus model gets contextWindow: 1000000 instead of 128000
- [x] Verified locally: with LiteLLM stopped, falls back to 128000 default gracefully

target.title: [Bug] cron remove: fix silent failure when job does not exist
target.body:
## Summary

The `cron remove` command (and `openclaw cron rm` / `openclaw cron delete` aliases) sometimes reports success but the job keeps running. This happens because the underlying `remove()` operation always returned `ok: true`, even when the job did not exist.

## Root Cause

In `src/cron/service/ops.ts`, the `remove()` function:

1. Always returned `{ ok: true, removed }` regardless of whether a job was found
2. Called `armTimer()` even when nothing was removed
3. Emitted a removed event only when `removed === true` — masking the problem from observability

The CLI layer printed the success message based solely on `ok: true` without checking `removed`.

## Fix

Updated `remove()` to return a richer result:

- `{ ok: false, removed: false, reason: 'store unavailable' }` — store not loaded
- `{ ok: false, removed: false, reason: 'job not found' }` — job did not exist
- `{ ok: true, removed: true, jobName: '...' }` — successfully removed

Also removed the spurious `armTimer()` call when nothing was removed.

## Verification

The `cron rm <id>`, `cron remove <id>`, and `cron delete <id>` CLI aliases all route through the same `cron.remove` gateway RPC call, so fixing the service layer fixes all three.

Fixes #28715

target.title: feat: add publicMode for public-facing agents
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds per-agent `publicMode` config flag that suppresses local runtime details (hostname, OS, architecture, workspace paths, skills snapshot) from the system prompt when serving public-facing agents through the HTTP API
- Introduces `X-OpenClaw-Sender-Is-Owner` header for `/v1/chat/completions` and `/v1/responses` endpoints, replacing the hardcoded `senderIsOwner: true` so HTTP callers can declare trust level
- For `publicMode` agents, missing header defaults to non-owner semantics; for normal agents, missing header defaults to owner semantics (preserving backward compatibility)

## Motivation

When exposing agents to untrusted external users (community bots, website chat widgets, public Telegram groups), the system prompt currently injects local runtime details like hostname, OS version, architecture, and workspace paths. This leaks infrastructure information to end users who interact through the HTTP API.

`publicMode` lets operators designate specific agents as public-facing. Those agents get a sanitized `"Runtime: public-facing agent"` line instead of the full runtime block, and their skills snapshot is suppressed. Combined with the `X-OpenClaw-Sender-Is-Owner: false` header, external-facing proxies can signal that requests come from untrusted users.

## Changes

| Area | Files | What |
|------|-------|------|
| Config | `types.agents.ts`, `zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`, `schema.help.ts` | `publicMode?: boolean` field + validation + help text |
| Agent scope | `agent-scope.ts` | `resolveAgentPublicMode()` config lookup |
| System prompt | `system-prompt.ts`, `system-prompt-params.ts` | Suppress host/OS/arch/workspace when publicMode; emit `"Runtime: public-facing agent"` |
| Skills | `commands-system-prompt.ts` | Suppress skills snapshot for publicMode agents |
| HTTP API | `http-utils.ts`, `openai-http.ts`, `openresponses-http.ts`, `server-http.ts` | `resolveIngressSenderIsOwner()` + header parsing + `runtimeConfig` threading |
| Runner | `cli-runner/helpers.ts`, `pi-embedded-runner/system-prompt.ts`, `pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` | Thread `publicMode` through CLI and embedded runners |
| Docs | `openai-http-api.md`, `openresponses-http-api.md`, `security/index.md` | Security boundary docs, header usage, config reference |
| Tests | 6 test files | System prompt suppression, param redaction, header parsing (67 tests pass) |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm build` passes (no errors)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` passes (no type errors)
- [x] `pnpm format` passes
- [x] Pre-commit hooks pass
- [x] 67 tests pass across 6 test files covering:
  - System prompt emits `"Runtime: public-facing agent"` when publicMode is true
  - System prompt params suppress host/OS/arch/repoRoot/shell when publicMode
  - Skills snapshot suppressed for publicMode agents
  - `resolveIngressSenderIsOwner` parses header correctly
  - Default senderIsOwner behavior: true for normal agents, false for publicMode agents
  - Header override works for both publicMode and normal agents

target.title: feat: add Anthropic native web search provider (#49949)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `"anthropic"` as a new web search provider option, enabling Anthropic's server-side `web_search_20260209` tool to be injected directly into the Messages API request. Claude handles search execution server-side with built-in citations, domain filtering, and prompt caching.

Closes #49949

## What changed

### New files
- **`extensions/anthropic/src/anthropic-web-search-provider.ts`** — Provider plugin implementing the `WebSearchProviderPlugin` interface. Resolves config (tool version, allowed/blocked domains, max uses, user location) and builds the raw Anthropic server tool definition.
- **`extensions/anthropic/src/anthropic-native-search-wrapper.ts`** — Stream wrapper that intercepts the `onPayload` callback to inject the server tool into the `tools` array for `anthropic-messages` API calls. Skips injection if a web_search server tool is already present.

### Modified files
- **`extensions/anthropic/index.ts`** — Registers the new web search provider.
- **`src/plugins/bundled-web-search.ts`** — Adds `"anthropic"` to the bundled web search plugin ID list.

### Tests (18 passing)
- **`anthropic-web-search.test.ts`** (15 tests) — Config resolution, tool version validation, server tool building (domains, max_uses, user_location, edge cases).
- **`anthropic-native-search-wrapper.test.ts`** (3 tests) — Injection into anthropic payloads, dedup when already present, passthrough for non-anthropic APIs.

## Configuration

```json5
{
  tools: {
    web: {
      search: {
        provider: "anthropic",
        anthropic: {
          toolVersion: "web_search_20260209",  // or "web_search_20250305"
          allowedDomains: ["example.com"],
          blockedDomains: ["untrusted.com"],
          maxUses: 5,
          userLocation: {
            type: "approximate",
            city: "Denver",
            region: "Colorado",
            country: "US",
            timezone: "America/Denver"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## How it works

Unlike other providers that intercept `web_search` tool calls and route them to external APIs, this provider uses Anthropic's **server tool** mechanism. The stream wrapper patches the API payload to include the `web_search_20260209` tool, and Claude executes searches server-side. Results come back as `server_tool_use` / `web_search_tool_result` content blocks with encrypted content and built-in citations.

No separate API key needed — uses the existing Anthropic API key.

target.title: feat(telegram): handle unknown groups with warn or leave
target.body:
## Summary

Add Telegram unknown-group handling with configurable actions:
- `unknownGroupAction`
- `unknownGroupMessage`
- `unknownGroupCooldownMs`

This lets OpenClaw ignore, warn, or leave unknown Telegram groups before assistant execution.

## What changed

- added Telegram config fields for unknown-group handling
- added Telegram runtime handling for:
  - `ignore`
  - `warn`
  - `leave`
- added cooldown protection for warning/leave behavior
- ensured forum-topic warning messages preserve `message_thread_id`
- fixed callback-query regression introduced during implementation
- added tests covering:
  - allowed groups unaffected
  - unknown group ignore
  - unknown group warn
  - unknown group leave
  - unknown forum-topic warn/leave behavior
  - callback flows remain intact

## Why

For private-assistant Telegram deployments, users may want:
- one explicitly approved private group to work normally
- no mention requirement in that trusted group
- unknown groups to be rejected by default
- optionally warn and leave unknown groups automatically

OpenClaw already has useful group-policy primitives, but it lacked a clear first-class behavior for unknown groups.

## Validation

Local verification included:
- focused and broader Telegram test coverage passing
- real Telegram behavior test where the bot posted the warning message and then left a throwaway unknown group

## Notes

This keeps behavior backward compatible by making the new config optional.

target.title: fix(googlechat): use agent identity.name in typing indicator fallback chain
target.body:
## Problem

Closes #49350

When a Google Chat agent has `identity.name` configured but no direct `agent.name`, the typing indicator was falling back to the hardcoded `"OpenClaw is typing..."` string instead of the configured identity name.

## Root Cause

`resolveBotDisplayName` in `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.ts` has a 3-step fallback chain:
1. Account config name (`account.config.name`)
2. Agent name (`agent.name`)
3. ❌ Hardcoded `"OpenClaw"`

It was missing a check for `agent.identity?.name`, which is the standard way users configure bot identity in OpenClaw (`IdentityConfig.name`).

## Fix

Add `agent.identity?.name` as a third fallback before the hardcoded `"OpenClaw"` string.

**Updated fallback chain:**
1. Account config name (`account.config.name`)
2. Agent name (`agent.name`)
3. ✅ Agent identity name (`agent.identity?.name`) ← **new**
4. `"OpenClaw"` as last resort

## Example

Config:
```json
{
  "agents": {
    "list": [{
      "id": "main",
      "identity": { "name": "Alvin" }
    }]
  }
}
```

**Before:** Shows `_OpenClaw is typing..._`
**After:** Shows `_Alvin is typing..._`

## Changes

- `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.ts`: Added `agent?.identity?.name` check in `resolveBotDisplayName` (5 lines)
- Updated JSDoc comment to reflect the 4-step fallback chain

target.title: fix(sessions): clean expired archive artifacts during maintenance
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: enforced session maintenance only cleaned archived deleted transcripts when the same save also archived a live-session removal, so stale `.deleted.*` artifacts could survive indefinitely in otherwise healthy stores.
- Why it matters: session directories keep accumulating expired deleted/reset artifacts even though the maintenance docs describe retention-based cleanup.
- What changed: always run deleted/reset archive cleanup against the store directory during enforced maintenance, and broaden archive timestamp parsing so older filename variants are still eligible for retention cleanup.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change live-session retention policy, lock timeouts, or add new maintenance knobs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #22506
- Related #48950

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Expired `.deleted.*` and `.reset.*` session archive artifacts are now cleaned up more reliably during enforced session maintenance, including legacy timestamp filename variants.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04
- Runtime/container: Node 22 source checkout
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a
- Relevant config (redacted): session maintenance `mode: enforce`

### Steps

1. Create a sessions store with enforced maintenance enabled.
2. Place an expired `.deleted.*` transcript and a fresh `.deleted.*` transcript in the store directory.
3. Save the session store without pruning any live sessions.

### Expected

- The expired archive is removed and the fresh archive remains.

### Actual

- Before this change, the expired archive could remain because cleanup only ran when the same save had already archived a deleted live session.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: ran a direct maintenance save against a temp sessions directory and confirmed an expired `.deleted.*` artifact was pruned while a recent artifact remained; verified legacy archive filename variants like `2026-02-23T12-34-56.000+00-00` and `20260223-123456` parse into retention timestamps.
- Edge cases checked: cleanup still runs when no live sessions were pruned, and legacy timestamp formats remain eligible for cleanup.
- What you did **not** verify: a long-lived production directory with thousands of archived files.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit; maintenance returns to the previous archive-cleanup behavior.
- Files/config to restore: `src/config/sessions/store.ts`, `src/config/sessions/artifacts.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected deletion of recent session archives or retention cleanup skipping legacy-named artifacts.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: broader timestamp parsing could classify an unexpected filename as an archive artifact.
  - Mitigation: parsing stays limited to the existing `.bak`, `.deleted`, and `.reset` suffix patterns and adds targeted tests for the accepted legacy formats.

target.title: fix(session-status): infer provider for bare model overrides
target.body:
## Summary

Fix model switching when the current session is on one provider (for example `openai-codex`) and the user selects a bare model name that belongs to a different provider (for example `deepseek-chat`).

Before this change, the `session_status` model override path used the current provider as the default provider for bare model names, so `deepseek-chat` could be incorrectly resolved as `openai-codex/deepseek-chat`, which then failed allowlist validation.

## Root cause

In `src/agents/tools/session-status-tool.ts`, `resolveModelOverride()` called `resolveModelRefFromString()` with `defaultProvider: currentProvider`.

That works for same-provider shorthand, but it breaks when:

- the current session provider is `openai-codex`
- the configured allowlist also includes `deepseek/deepseek-chat`
- the user switches using the bare model name `deepseek-chat`

The bare model was resolved against the wrong provider and rejected as:

`model not allowed: openai-codex/deepseek-chat`

## Fix

- extract the raw model portion with `splitTrailingAuthProfile()`
- when the selection is a bare model name (not alias, not `provider/model`), try `inferUniqueProviderFromConfiguredModels()` against the configured allowlist
- use the inferred provider when available, otherwise fall back to the current provider

This preserves existing same-provider shorthand behavior while allowing cross-provider switches when the target model can be uniquely inferred from config.

## Tests

Added a regression test covering:

- current override: `openai-codex/gpt-5.3-codex`
- allowlisted target: `deepseek/deepseek-chat`
- user input: `deepseek-chat`
- expected stored override: `deepseek/deepseek-chat`

Ran:

- `corepack pnpm exec vitest src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`

## Notes

This bug is especially visible for auth-backed `openai-codex` setups, because users often switch away from Codex to other configured providers using short model names in the model picker / session override flow.

target.title: fix(ui): keep delete confirm in viewport
target.body:
## Related issue

Related #50277

## Summary

- Position the chat delete confirmation popover relative to the viewport instead of the scroll container.
- Choose above or below the delete button based on available vertical space.
- Clamp the popover horizontally so it remains reachable on narrow viewports.
- Add focused jsdom geometry coverage for top-edge, bottom-edge, and narrow-width placement.

## User-visible behavior

The delete confirmation popover in Control UI chat should remain visible and reachable when deleting messages near the top or edges of the chat viewport.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI grouped chat delete confirmation could render out of reach when opened near the top or horizontal viewport edges because it was absolutely positioned inside the scrollable chat area. This patch positions the popover against the viewport, flips it above or below the delete button, and clamps it inside viewport margins.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `4a0465007c` on Raspberry Pi-class Linux host, Node `v22.21.1`, Chromium `142.0.7444.175`, using the PR's `.chat-delete-confirm` markup/CSS and viewport-placement code path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
chromium --headless=new --disable-gpu --no-sandbox --force-device-scale-factor=1 --window-size=360,640 --dump-dom file:///tmp/openclaw-delete-confirm-proof.html
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Terminal output excerpt from Chromium:

```text
OpenClaw Control UI delete-confirm viewport proof
viewport=500x553
top-edge: placement=below left=90 top=38 withinViewport=true
bottom-edge: placement=above left=12 top=86 withinViewport=true
horizontal-edge: placement=below left=104 top=130 withinViewport=true
```

- Observed result after fix: In Chromium, the delete confirmation popover remains inside the viewport margins for top-edge, bottom-edge, and horizontal-edge cases. The top-edge case opens below the trigger, the bottom-edge case opens above the trigger, and the horizontal-edge case stays within the right boundary.
- What was not tested: Full gateway-backed dashboard click path on this Raspberry Pi-class host; dependency installation was intentionally avoided. Visual screenshot capture was not attached because copied browser output is the supplied proof.

## Supplemental validation

- `git diff --check` exited 0 locally with no whitespace errors.
- GitHub Actions `checks-node-core-ui` passed on head `4a0465007c`.
- GitHub Actions `check-test-types` passed on head `4a0465007c` after adding `this: HTMLElement` to the test geometry mock.

## AI-assisted

AI-assisted: yes. I reviewed the diff and understand the change scope.

target.title: fix(telegram): add block streaming coalesce defaults to prevent message splitting
target.body:
## Summary

- Telegram was the only channel plugin missing `blockStreamingCoalesceDefaults` in its streaming config
- Every other block-streaming channel (Slack, Discord, Mattermost, Google Chat, Signal) sets `{ minChars: 1500, idleMs: 1000 }` so the block reply coalescer buffers small streamed fragments before flushing them as separate messages
- Without these defaults, Telegram fell back to the global minimum (800 chars / 1 s idle), causing short streamed paragraphs to be sent as individual messages instead of being merged into a single message

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue

Fixes #47454

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Multi-line Telegram responses are now properly coalesced into fewer messages during block streaming, matching the behavior of other channels.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Test plan

- [x] Existing `chunk.test.ts` (31 tests), `deliver.test.ts` (43 tests), `draft-chunking.test.ts` (3 tests), and `block-streaming.test.ts` (3 tests) all pass
- [ ] Manual verification: send a multi-line response to a Telegram bot — should arrive as a single message instead of being split per paragraph

target.title: fix(security): reject private/internal hosts in cron webhook URLs
target.body:
## Problem

`normalizeHttpWebhookUrl` only validated that webhook URLs used http(s) protocol, but did not check whether the hostname pointed to private, loopback, link-local, or other special-use addresses. A cron job configured with `delivery.mode="webhook"` could target:

- `localhost` / `127.0.0.1` / `[::1]`
- RFC 1918 private ranges (`10.x`, `172.16.x`, `192.168.x`)
- Cloud metadata endpoints (`169.254.169.254`, `metadata.google.internal`)
- `.local` / `.internal` hostnames

While the runtime fetch layer performs DNS-pinned SSRF validation, rejecting these at URL creation time prevents dangerous URLs from being persisted in cron job configs in the first place.

## Fix

Import and apply the existing `isBlockedHostnameOrIp` guard from the SSRF module (`src/infra/net/ssrf.ts`) inside `normalizeHttpWebhookUrl`. This provides defense-in-depth: blocked URLs are rejected early during cron job creation/patching, before any network request is attempted.

## Testing

- Added `src/cron/webhook-url.test.ts` with 15 test cases covering protocol validation, SSRF hostname/IP rejection, and public URL acceptance
- Verified existing `service.jobs.test.ts` (35 tests) and `normalize.test.ts` (30 tests) continue to pass
- [x] AI-assisted, fully tested
- [x] Understand what the code does

target.title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized
correctly in channel relay setups.
- Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly
allowed.
- What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the
receiving account.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`,
because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #49062

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats.
- Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."`
- Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The
risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied
channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13
Operating system: all OS
Install method: npm global
Model: gpt-5.4
Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth



### Steps

1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed.
2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel.
3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior.

### Expected

- Explicitly allowed channel chats can execute native commands such as /status.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the command was rejected or dropped on channel_post.
- After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - /status from an explicitly allowed Telegram channel now succeeds.
    - Disallowed channel chats remain blocked by chat allowlist checks.
- Edge cases checked:
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not block explicitly allowed channel_post commands.
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not bypass denied channel_post chat allowlists.
- What you did not verify:
    - Sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom semantics for channel_post
    - Live plugin command behavior on Telegram channels
    - Webhook-specific Telegram deployment paths

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for
maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
    - Revert this PR, or disable Telegram native commands temporarily with channels.telegram.commands.native: false
- Files/config to restore:
    - extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - Explicitly allowed Telegram channel /status still returns "You are not authorized to use this command."
    - Disallowed channel chats start executing native commands unexpectedly

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write None.

- Risk:
    - channel_post authorization now uses explicit chat allow rather than sender-scoped identity.
    - Mitigation:
        - This is limited to Telegram channel_post, where Telegram does not expose the originating human sender.
        - Explicit chat allow is still required.
        - Regression tests cover both allow and deny behavior.
- Risk:
    - Future contributors may assume sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom also applies to channel_post.
    - Mitigation:
        - Scope is explicitly documented in this PR.
        - A separate follow-up issue will track channel_post sender-scoped allowlist semantics.

target.title: test(config): keep agent model overrides selection-only
target.body:
## Summary
- keep `agents.list[].model` on the same selection-only schema as other agent model overrides
- keep `agents.list[].subagents.model` selection-only as well
- add regression coverage so `timeoutMs` stays limited to the tool/media model config surfaces that actually consume it

Follow-up to #83339. Supersedes the original timeout-support direction for #83291.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: agent and subagent model override config should not silently accept `timeoutMs`; timeout config belongs on the tool/media model schemas that consume it.
- Real environment tested: Windows OpenClaw worktree rebased on `origin/main` at `cb408bb06b`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
CI=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/schema.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot
git diff --check
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  43 passed (43)
```

- Observed result after fix: the schema test passes while rejecting `agents.list[0].model.timeoutMs` and `agents.list[0].subagents.model.timeoutMs`.
- What was not tested: live agent runtime execution, because this PR only locks the config schema contract.

## Root Cause
The original PR direction treated model selection objects like tool model config objects. Current main now separates those contracts: model selection chooses a model (`primary`/`fallbacks`), while timeout values belong to surfaces that own timeout behavior.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level: focused config schema unit test.
- Target file: `src/config/schema.test.ts`.
- Scenario locked in: per-agent model and subagent model overrides reject `timeoutMs`.

## Validation
- `CI=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/schema.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: feat: Feishu message recovery module
target.body:
## Issue
解决 #28218: Feishu: fetch missed messages on reconnect

## 问题描述
当网关离线时（如电脑睡眠），飞书消息丢失。飞书 WebSocket 不像 Telegram 会重放离线消息。

## 解决方案
添加消息恢复服务，定期检查群聊消息，发现并恢复丢失的消息。

## 变更内容
新增文件：
- extensions/feishu/src/feishu-message-recovery.ts - 消息恢复模块

## 功能特性
- 定期检查消息（每30秒）
- 自动恢复丢失的消息
- 消息处理统计
- 支持手动触发恢复

target.title: test(gateway): consolidate deriveSessionTitle tests into dedicated module
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes an issue where heartbeat polls could overwrite explicit user-labeled session titles with transcript-derived titles like `"heartbeat"`.

## Changes

- Extracts `deriveSessionTitle` to `session-title.ts` with a dedicated test module.
- Preserves explicit session labels before transcript-derived fallbacks.
- Keeps auto-populated `origin.label` behind `firstUserMessage` so unlabeled inbound DMs can still use transcript-derived titles.
- Keeps `session-utils.ts` as a compatibility re-export site.
- Clarifies the zero timestamp test name to describe the actual `updatedAt: 0` input.

## Title precedence

1. `displayName`
2. `subject`
3. explicit `entry.label`
4. `firstUserMessage` (truncated)
5. auto-populated `entry.origin?.label`
6. `sessionId` prefix fallback

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: explicit session labels should survive heartbeat-style transcript summaries, while auto-populated origin labels should not override a real first user message.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on this branch after rebasing onto current `main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --eval 'import { deriveSessionTitle } from "./src/gateway/session-title.ts";
const cases = {
  explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), label: "My App - Dashboard" }, "heartbeat"),
  transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, "Need help with deployment"),
  originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, null),
  zeroUpdatedAtFallback: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abcd1234-5678-90ef-ghij-klmnopqrstuv", updatedAt: 0 }),
};
console.log(JSON.stringify(cases, null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat": "My App - Dashboard",
  "transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel": "Need help with deployment",
  "originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript": "Discord: Engineering",
  "zeroUpdatedAtFallback": "abcd1234"
}
```

Observed result after fix: explicit labels win over heartbeat transcript text; transcript text wins over auto-populated origin labels; origin labels still act as fallback when there is no transcript; zero `updatedAt` keeps the session-id-only fallback.

What was not tested: full gateway UI rendering was not manually opened; this proof covers the extracted title derivation behavior directly.

## Validation

- `pnpm test src/gateway/session-title.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

target.title: ACP: add gateway-owned node-backed runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker.
- Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove.
- What this PR adds:
  - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints
  - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes
  - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing
  - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator
  - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs
  - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage
  - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend
- Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes.

## What changed by area

### 1) Durable ACP gateway state

New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`:

- persists ACP sessions and runs
- persists accepted worker events
- tracks leases / lease epochs
- resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway
- stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor

This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node.

### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/**`
- `src/infra/node-commands.ts`
- `src/node-host/**`

This adds the node-backed ACP execution path:

- gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node
- node-host can run the ACP worker role
- worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport
- gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution

### 3) Durable reply projection and replay

Main implementation areas:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`

This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path:

- reply targets are persisted per run
- projector checkpoints are advanced only after successful delivery
- restart recovery can replay the unfinished suffix from durable state
- reconnect and duplicate accepted events do not re-deliver already completed outward effects

### 4) Startup recovery / reconnect handling

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
- `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`

This adds the gateway-side recovery flow needed to:

- resume incomplete projection after restart
- keep reconnect handling deterministic
- prevent stale-worker wins
- preserve canonical terminal behavior across restart/replay windows

### 5) Test harnesses and regression coverage

Main test additions/expansions:

- `src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts`
- `src/acp/test-harness/restart-harness.ts`

These cover the core failure windows this backend needs to survive:

- restart after accepted events but before replay checkpoint advancement
- restart after terminal persistence but before final outward convergence
- reconnect replay of already accepted events
- cancel / complete / close race handling
- restart recovery with a real memory cut instead of reusing in-process objects

### 6) Docs included in this PR

Main docs:

- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-verification.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-runtime-program.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-summary.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-final-judge.md`

These are included because this was developed as a staged architecture / verification effort and the final accepted behavior is documented there.

## Explicit scope boundaries / v1 limitations

This PR does **not**:

- turn nodes into mini-gateways
- add automatic cross-node continuation for an in-flight run
- claim durable resumed tool-message edit support after restart

Current conservative post-restart tool-update behavior remains:

- `append_only_after_restart`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: ACP node runtime architecture / protocol / verification docs under `docs/experiments/plans/`
- Related: landing-only extension test isolation fixes after rebase onto current `main`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenClaw now has a first-class ACP node-backed runtime path where the gateway owns durable ACP truth and paired nodes execute the worker role.
- Gateway restart/recovery can resume incomplete ACP outward projection from durable state.
- Duplicate accepted worker-event replay on reconnect is hardened so it does not duplicate outward delivery.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally follow the documented conservative v1 rule: `append_only_after_restart`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? Yes

### Security / trust-boundary notes

- Paired nodes can now advertise ACP worker capability and receive ACP runtime commands over the existing gateway↔node trust boundary.
- Gateway↔node websocket traffic now carries ACP worker control / event flows in addition to existing node commands.
- Gateway now persists ACP runtime state needed for durable replay/recovery.
- The design mitigates stale-worker / duplicate-delivery risks via lease epochs, canonical terminal resolution, durable replay checkpoints, and reconnect/replay handling on the gateway.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (landing worktree on vm1) + GitHub Actions CI
- Runtime/container: Node / pnpm; Bun build path also checked
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP gateway↔node runtime path
- Relevant config (redacted): ACP runtime restart/replay harnesses and paired-node fixtures

### Verified locally

- ACP targeted verification gate:
  - `pnpm test -- src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts src/acp/store/store.test.ts src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts src/acp/store/gateway-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.acp.test.ts`
- TypeScript guardrail:
  - `pnpm tsgo`
- Landing-only extension test fixes:
  - `pnpm test:extensions`

### CI / build notes

- `pnpm build` still fails only with the pre-existing Bun wrapper issue:
  - `Cannot find module './cjs/index.cjs' from ''`
- That build failure was already present and is not introduced by the ACP runtime changes in this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the ACP-focused local gate listed above.
- Verified `pnpm tsgo` passes.
- Verified the rebased landing branch’s extension test-isolation fixes with local `pnpm test:extensions`.
- Verified the PR now includes the architecture / protocol / verification / final-judge docs for this backend.

What I did **not** verify:

- I did **not** get a clean `pnpm build` because of the pre-existing Bun wrapper failure noted above.
- I did **not** run a fresh manual end-to-end Discord/real-node interactive repro outside the checked-in restart/replay and node-host harnesses.

## Review Guide

If you want the shortest path through this PR, review in this order:

1. **Core architecture / intent**
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
2. **Gateway-owned durable state**
   - `src/acp/store/**`
3. **Node execution bridge**
   - `src/node-host/**`
   - `src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
4. **Durable outward replay path**
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`
5. **Recovery and proofs**
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
6. **Ignore as landing-only test fixes unless you are checking CI fallout**
   - `extensions/feishu/**`
   - `extensions/google/**`
   - `extensions/googlechat/**`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No explicit migration required
- Migration needed? No

Notes:

- This adds a new ACP runtime/backend path; it does not force existing deployments to switch to it immediately.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally remain append-only in v1.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR or avoid selecting/using the `acp-node` runtime path until fixed.
- Files/config to restore: ACP runtime/store/gateway/node-host changes under `src/acp/**`, `src/node-host/**`, `src/gateway/**`, plus the accompanying docs if reverting wholesale.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate final delivery after restart, stale worker winning after reconnect, projector checkpoint drift, or replay targeting the wrong route.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: restart/replay correctness is subtle in any durable projection system.
  - Mitigation: this PR includes restart harnesses, startup recovery tests, reconnect/replay ordering tests, and explicit protocol/verification docs.
- Risk: the PR is large and spans multiple ACP runtime layers.
  - Mitigation: the review guide above splits it into durable store, node bridge, replay path, recovery path, and landing-only CI fixes.
- Risk: paired-node ACP execution expands the gateway↔node command surface.
  - Mitigation: the gateway remains authoritative for leases, terminal resolution, checkpoints, and replay instead of trusting node-local completion.

---

## AI Checklist

- [x] **AI-assisted** — agent-harness / Codex driven implementation, review, and landing follow-ups
- [x] **Testing degree** — focused local verification plus CI
- [x] **Understanding confirmed** — this PR adds the gateway-owned ACP node-backed runtime and includes a few landing-only extension test fixes after rebase
- [ ] Prompts/session logs — available on request

target.title: feat(status): add API call count to session status and usage footer
target.body:

- persist per-run call count on session entries
- show current-turn call count in /status output

- keep calls semantics aligned with current-turn token usage
- add/update unit tests for API call count display

## Summary

- **Problem**: Users cannot see how many API calls were made per turn, only token usage
- **Why it matters**: Some providers charge per call; helps debug usage patterns and understand tool-call loops
- **What changed**: Added `callCount` tracking in the embedded runner, persisted to session entry, displayed in both `/status` and response usage footer

target.title: fix(feishu): send p2p replies to user targets
target.body:
Fixes #83730.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
- Feishu p2p final replies were being dispatched to the raw p2p `chat_id` (`oc_*`) after reply-to delivery is skipped for DMs.
- The inbound path already computes the safer sender target as `user:<open_id>`, so this sends p2p final delivery through that target while preserving group/topic delivery through `chat:<chat_id>`.

Real environment tested:
- Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS.
- Production Feishu message routing path exercised with Vitest using the real `handleFeishuMessage` dispatcher setup.
- No live Feishu tenant credentials were used; the proof validates the target passed into the Feishu send dispatcher immediately before network delivery.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts -t "routes p2p final delivery"
```

Evidence after fix:
```console
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 69 skipped (70)
```

The focused regression asserts:
```text
p2p dispatcher chatId = user:ou_sender_1
p2p replyToMessageId = msg-p2p-target
p2p skipReplyToInMessages = true
```

Additional target mapping proof:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node --import tsx -e 'import { resolveReceiveIdType } from "./extensions/feishu/src/targets.ts"; console.log(JSON.stringify({ dmTarget: "user:ou_sender_1", dmReceiveIdType: resolveReceiveIdType("user:ou_sender_1"), groupTarget: "chat:oc-group", groupReceiveIdType: resolveReceiveIdType("chat:oc-group") }, null, 2));'
{
  "dmTarget": "user:ou_sender_1",
  "dmReceiveIdType": "open_id",
  "groupTarget": "chat:oc-group",
  "groupReceiveIdType": "chat_id"
}
```

Observed result after fix:
- p2p final reply dispatch targets `user:ou_sender_1`, which maps to Feishu `open_id` delivery.
- p2p reply-to suppression is preserved (`skipReplyToInMessages=true`) so the fix does not re-enable the failing direct reply-to path.
- Existing group/topic behavior remains on `chat:oc-group`, mapping to Feishu `chat_id`.

Full validation:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts

Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  179 passed (179)
```

Other checks:
```console
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts
git diff --check
git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD
```
